$comment: Literary clock data for <quoting-clock>. Keys are the 96 quarter-hour slots, 'HH:MM' in 24-hour time. Entry schema: { quote, title, author } — store the quote without surrounding quotation marks; the component supplies them. Slots may be sparse: missing slots fall back to the nearest populated one. TODO(srosebattles): curate the full 96 slots and verify wording against the source texts (see README).

00:00.quote: Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary, / Over many a quaint and curious volume of forgotten lore—
00:00.title: The Raven
00:00.author: Edgar Allan Poe

01:00.quote: Expect the first to-morrow, when the bell tolls One.
01:00.title: A Christmas Carol
01:00.author: Charles Dickens

07:15.quote: He was a late riser, as a rule, and as the clock on the mantelpiece showed me that it was only a quarter-past seven, I blinked up at him in some surprise.
07:15.title: The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes
07:15.author: Arthur Conan Doyle

11:30.quote: Phileas Fogg, having shut the door of his house at half-past eleven, and having put his right foot before his left five hundred and seventy-five times, and his left foot before his right five hundred and seventy-six times, reached the Reform Club.
11:30.title: Around the World in Eighty Days
11:30.author: Jules Verne

12:00.quote: It was precisely twelve o’clock; twelve by Big Ben; whose stroke was wafted over the northern part of London; blent with that of other clocks, mixed in a thin ethereal way with the clouds and wisps of smoke, and died up there among the seagulls.
12:00.title: Mrs Dalloway
12:00.author: Virginia Woolf

16:30.quote: Under certain circumstances there are few hours in life more agreeable than the hour dedicated to the ceremony known as afternoon tea.
16:30.title: The Portrait of a Lady
16:30.author: Henry James

20:30.quote: Left Munich at 8:35 P. M., on 1st May, arriving at Vienna early next morning; should have arrived at 6:46, but train was an hour late.
20:30.title: Dracula
20:30.author: Bram Stoker
